title: On the Genealogy of Morality
description: On the Genealogy of Morality — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: "Front Matter"
memes.description: "Title page and edition information for Nietzsche's \"On the Genealogy of Morality\""

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §1"
memes.description: "The Europeans now imagine themselves as representing, in the main, the highest types of men on earth."

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §10"
memes.description: 'We could at any time have counted on the fingers of one hand those German learned men who possessed wit: the remainder…'

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §11"
memes.description: "As Frenchmen reflect the politeness and esprit of French society, so do Germans reflect something of the deep, pensive…"

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §12"
memes.description: "A larger number of the higher and better-endowed men will, I hope, have in the end so much self-restraint as to be able…"

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §13"
memes.description: "The peasant is the commonest type of noblesse, for he is dependent upon himself most of all."

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §14"
memes.description: "The future of German culture rests with the sons of the Prussian officers."

memes.description: "There has always been a want of wit in Germany, and mediocre heads attain there to the highest honours, because even…"

memes.description: 'Parliaments may be very useful to a strong and versatile statesman: he has something there to rely upon (every such…'

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §17"
memes.description: "Can any one interest himself in this German Empire? Where is the new thought? Is it only a new combination of power?"

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §18"
memes.description: 'I see over and beyond all these national wars, new "empires," and whatever else lies in the foreground.'

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §19"
memes.description: "The condition of Europe in the next century will once again lead to the breeding of manly virtues, because men will…"

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §2"
memes.description: 'A characteristic of Europeans: inconsistency between word and deed; the Oriental is true to himself in daily life.'

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §20"
memes.description: "What quagmires and mendacity must there be about if it is possible, in the modern European hotch-potch, to raise…"

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §21"
memes.description: 'Maxim: To associate with no man who takes any part in the mendacious race swindle.'

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §22"
memes.description: "With the freedom of travel now existing, groups of men of the same kindred can join together and establish communal…"

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §23"
memes.description: "To make Europe a centre of culture, national stupidities should not make us blind to the fact that in the higher…"

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §24"
memes.description: "Mankind has still much before it—how, generally speaking, could the ideal be taken from the past?"

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §25"
memes.description: "This is our distrust, which recurs again and again; our care, which never lets us sleep; our question, which no one…"

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §26"
memes.description: "Genius and Epoch.—Heroism is no form of selfishness, for one is shipwrecked by it...."

memes.description: 'The fate now overhanging Europe is simply this: that it is exactly her strongest sons that come rarely and late to the…'

memes.description: "I rate Michael Angelo higher than Raphael, because, through all the Christian clouds and prejudices of his time, he saw…"

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §4"
memes.description: "How much we have learnt and learnt anew in fifty years!"

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §5"
memes.description: "What I observe with pleasure in the German is his Mephistophelian nature; but, to tell the truth, one must have a…"

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §6"
memes.description: "Perhaps the Germans have only grown up in a wrong climate!"

memes.label: "Peoples and Countries, §7"
memes.description: 'The Germans are a dangerous people: they are experts at inventing intoxicants.'

memes.description: "The smallness and baseness of the German soul were not and are not consequences of the system of small states; for it…"

memes.description: "Most artists, even some of the greatest (including the historians) have up to the present belonged to the serving…"

memes.label: "Preface, Section 1"
memes.description: 'We are unknown, we knowers, ourselves to ourselves: this has its own good reason.'

memes.label: "Preface, Section 5"
memes.description: "In reality I had set my heart at that time on something much more important than the nature of the theories of myself…"

memes.label: "Preface, Section 8"

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: Anti-Semites
memes.description: 'In Nietzsche''s Genealogy, the anti-Semites appear as a case study in modern ressentiment — a movement whose professed idealism masks the same psychological mechanism of reactive hatred that Nietzsche diagnoses at the heart of slave morality itself.'

memes.label: Aristocracy
memes.description: 'In Nietzsche''s Genealogy, the aristocracy is the "master" caste whose spontaneous self-affirmation creates the original value-equation (good = noble = powerful = beautiful), against which all later moral systems react.'

memes.label: Arnold Geulincx
memes.description: 'Flemish Cartesian philosopher whose concept of "despectus sui" (self-contempt) is cited by Nietzsche in the Genealogy as an expression of the ascetic ideal s psychological mechanism.'

memes.label: Arthur Schopenhauer
memes.description: 'German philosopher (1788–1860) whose pessimism and concept of the will profoundly influenced Nietzsche, especially in his early work and continuing through the Genealogy.'

memes.label: "Artist"
memes.description: 'The role and psychology of the artist in relation to ascetic ideals, as analyzed by Nietzsche in Essay 3'

memes.label: Ascetic Ideal
memes.description: 'The central concept of Nietzsche''s Third Essay: the ascetic ideal is the fundamental interpretative framework that has given human suffering meaning through self-denial, operating across religion, philosophy, and art.'

memes.label: "Ascetic Priest"
memes.description: 'The ascetic priest as the guardian and propagator of the ascetic ideal: the herdsman of the sick herd who redirects ressentiment inward through the production of guilt'

memes.label: Bad Conscience
memes.description: 'A core Nietzschean concept from the Second Essay: the internalization

memes.label: Baruch Spinoza
memes.description: 'Dutch-Jewish rationalist philosopher (1632–1677) whom Nietzsche acknowledges in the Genealogy as a rare philosophical predecessor — an honest atheist whose concept of "sympathia malevolens" (interested malice) and whose intellectual courage in facing the problem of suffering anticipated key elements of Nietzsche''s own genealogical method.'

memes.label: Blaise Pascal
memes.description: 'French philosopher, mathematician, and Jansenist theologian (1623–1662) whose principle "il faut s''abêtir" — one must make oneself stupid — is cited by Nietzsche in the Third Essay as the psychological essence of religious asceticism: the deliberate reduction of consciousness to escape the pain of existence.'

memes.label: Buddha
memes.description: 'Founder of Buddhism (c. 5th–4th century BCE) — Nietzsche invokes Buddha twice in the Genealogy: as the philosopher who equated family life with bondage, and as the endpoint of an Indian philosophical development that parallels European nihilism.'

memes.label: Charles Darwin
memes.description: 'English naturalist (1809–1882) whose theory of evolution by natural selection is treated skeptically in the Preface to the Genealogy — Nietzsche critiques "the Darwinian beast" as a creature of English theoretical optimism, not the genealogical reality of human moral history.'

memes.label: Christianity
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s critique of Christianity as the historical vehicle of the slave revolt in morality — the religion that perfected the revaluation of values, universalized the bad conscience, and elevated the ascetic ideal to its most destructive form'

memes.label: Church
memes.description: 'In Nietzsche''s Genealogy, the Church appears as the institutional embodiment of slave morality — an organization that Nietzsche characterizes as a "hospital for the sick" that preserves and propagates the ressentiment-driven revaluation of values.'

memes.label: Conscience
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s analysis of conscience (Gewissen) as the internalized voice of morality, encompassing both the "bad conscience" born of repressed cruelty and the "intellectual conscience" prized by the free spirit'

memes.label: Contempt
memes.description: 'In the Genealogy, contempt is an affect with two opposing modalities — the noble outward contempt that affirms strength and distance, and the ascetic self-contempt that turns the will to power inward against the self.'

memes.label: Creditor-Debtor Relationship
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s genealogical thesis that the concepts of guilt, duty, and justice originate in the contractual relationship between creditor and debtor — the oldest form of interpersonal obligation and the source of the moral vocabulary of Schuld'

memes.label: Crucifixion of Jesus
memes.description: 'In the Genealogy, the crucifixion of Jesus is the supreme symbol of the slave revolt in morals — the event through which the "self-crucifixion of God" for human guilt becomes the most powerful instrument of the ascetic ideal.'

memes.label: Cruelty
memes.description: 'In the Genealogy, cruelty is not a pathology but a fundamental human instinct — the joy in inflicting and witnessing suffering — which civilization has not eliminated but internalized, transforming into bad conscience, guilt, and the ascetic ideal.'

memes.label: "Dante Alighieri"
memes.description: 'Italian poet whose inscription over the gate of Hell Nietzsche critiques in Essay 1 as an example of Christian theology masking hatred as love.'

memes.label: Debt
memes.description: In Nietzsche's genealogy, the creditor-debtor relationship is the primordial

memes.label: Disease
memes.description: 'In the Genealogy, disease is Nietzsche''s central metaphor for the pathological condition of man — moral systems, particularly Christianity, are analyzed as symptoms, contagions, and therapeutic failures of a fundamentally diseased animal.'

memes.label: "Enemy"
memes.description: 'The dual concept of the enemy in the genealogy of morality: the noble enemy as object of respect vs. the evil enemy as projection of slave ressentiment'

memes.label: England
memes.description: 'In Nietzsche''s Genealogy, England symbolizes modern moral shallowness — the home of utilitarian ethics and "English psychologists" whose reductive genealogies of morality Nietzsche attacks as the opposite of his own project.'

memes.label: Epicurus
memes.description: 'Ancient Greek philosopher (341–270 BC) whose concept of ataraxia (painlessness) as the highest good is cited by Nietzsche in his analysis of the ascetic ideal and the meaning of suffering.'

memes.label: "Ernest Renan"
memes.description: 'French historian and philologist whom Nietzsche excoriates as "half-priest, half-satyr," a representative of the "contemplative" school of historiography that disguises nihilistic impotence behind the posture of objectivity.'

memes.label: Eugen Dühring
memes.description: 'German philosopher and economist (1833–1921), "the first apostle of revenge in Germany" — Nietzsche uses Dühring as the exemplary case of ressentiment-driven thought, the "anti-historical" thinker who, for all his flaws, is nevertheless preferred to the "contemplative" historians.'

memes.label: Europe
memes.description: 'Europe in Nietzsche''s Genealogy is the stage upon which the entire drama of moral history unfolds — from the aristocratic cultures of antiquity through the slave revolt in morals to the modern crisis of nihilism.'

memes.label: "Felix Mendelssohn"
memes.description: 'German composer mentioned by Nietzsche as exemplifying artistic distinction akin to Goethe'

memes.label: Forgetfulness
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s concept of active forgetfulness as a positive, healthy faculty — not mere inertia or inability to remember, but an active power of psychic digestion essential to human health and the capacity for action'

memes.label: Frédéric Chopin
memes.description: 'Polish composer and virtuoso pianist (1810–1849) — Nietzsche cites Chopin as an example of the artist who "possesses distinction," a quality of natural aristocratic bearing in music shared with Van Dyck, Mendelssohn, and Goethe.'

memes.label: "Frederick the Great"
memes.description: 'King of Prussia invoked by Nietzsche as an embodiment of the "true German Mephistopheles" — dangerous, bold, wicked, and open-hearted.'

memes.label: Freedom
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s concept of the ''instinct of freedom'' (Instinkt der Freiheit) as the fundamental drive underlying punishment, bad conscience, and the will to power — the active force that resists all constraint and seeks discharge, whether outward against others or inward against the self'

memes.label: Friedrich Nietzsche
memes.description: 'German philosopher (1844–1900), author of "On the Genealogy of Morality" (Zur Genealogie der Moral, 1887), in which he develops the method of genealogical critique and the concepts of master morality, slave morality, ressentiment, and the ascetic ideal'

memes.label: "Genealogy"
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s genealogical method: the historical-physiological investigation of the origins of moral values that exposes their contingent, interested, and often brutal foundations'

memes.label: Germans
memes.description: 'In Nietzsche''s Genealogy, the Germans appear as a recurring object of diagnosis — a people whose "depth," dangerousness, and inner turmoil Nietzsche analyzes as symptomatic of the broader European moral crisis, while contrasting the "old Germans" of barbarian nobility with modern German cultural decay.'

memes.label: Germany
memes.description: 'Germany in the Genealogy is the site of Nietzsche''s most pointed cultural critique — a nation whose "best blood" produced figures like Goethe and Luther, yet whose modern condition exemplifies ressentiment, moral vulgarity, and cultural decline.'

memes.label: "Gods"
memes.description: 'The genealogical origin of gods in the creditor-debtor relationship: fear of ancestors, the growth of divinity from guilt, and the gradual moralization of the divine'

memes.label: Good and Evil
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s genealogical analysis of the moral opposition "good and evil" as a slave-moral revaluation of the earlier aristocratic "good and bad," born from the ressentiment of the weak against the strong'

memes.label: "Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz"
memes.description: 'German rationalist philosopher whom Nietzsche lists among the German "intoxicants."'

memes.label: Greece
memes.description: 'In Nietzsche''s Genealogy, Greece is the geographical and cultural origin of the aristocratic value system — the land whose myths, gods, and noble castes created the original equation of "good" with "powerful" before the slave revolt in morals.'

memes.label: Greeks
memes.description: 'In Nietzsche''s Genealogy, the ancient Greeks serve as the exemplary instance of pre-Christian nobility — a civilization whose gods, festivals, and moral sensibility demonstrate the healthy, outward-directed form of human existence that slave morality would later pathologize.'

memes.label: Guilt
memes.description: 'In the Genealogy, guilt (Schuld) originates in the material concept of debt (Schulden) — a genealogical link that Nietzsche uses to demystify morality and reveal its origins in the creditor-debtor relationship.'

memes.label: "Health"
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s concept of health as a physiological-psychological condition that determines the character of values, with the opposition between health and sickness driving the history of morality'

memes.label: Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel
memes.description: 'German idealist philosopher (1770–1831) whom Nietzsche treats as a symptom of German intellectual danger and a necessary antagonist for philosophical vitality.'

memes.label: "Heinrich Heine"
memes.description: 'German poet and essayist whom Nietzsche names among the "broad-minded and deep-thinking men" working toward a United Europe, contrasting their vision with the national narrowness of mere patriotism.'

memes.label: "Henry Thomas Buckle"
memes.description: 'English historian whose methodological assumptions Nietzsche critiques in Essay 1 as emblematic of the democratic prejudice infecting modern historical and ethical inquiry.'

memes.label: "Herbert Spencer"
memes.description: 'English philosopher whose utilitarian ethics and theory of adaptation Nietzsche critiques in the Genealogy as representative of the English psychological school.'

memes.label: Hippolyte Taine
memes.description: 'French critic, historian, and philosopher (1828–1893) — Nietzsche invokes Taine as the exemplar of the fearless psychologist, one who writes with "the fearlessness... that springs from force of character and not from a prudent toleration of force," contrasted with the clever opportunism of Leopold Ranke.'

memes.label: Homer
memes.description: 'Ancient Greek epic poet, repeatedly invoked by Nietzsche as the archetype of noble (master) morality and the creator of the Homeric hero ideal'

memes.label: Honoré Gabriel Mirabeau
memes.description: 'French revolutionary statesman (1749–1791) whose prodigious capacity for forgetting insults exemplifies, for Nietzsche in the Genealogy, the psychological health of the aristocratic type — a man "only incapable of forgiving because he forgot."'

memes.label: "Ideals"
memes.description: 'The fabrication and function of ideals in Nietzsche''s genealogy: how ideals are manufactured, what they serve, and the distinction between noble and ascetic ideals'

memes.label: Immanuel Kant
memes.description: 'German Enlightenment philosopher (1724–1804) whose categorical imperative and moral rationalism represent, for Nietzsche in the Genealogy, the most sophisticated philosophical articulation of slave morality — a "pious fraud" that disguises the will to power of the weak as universal law.'

memes.label: India
memes.description: 'In Nietzsche''s Genealogy, India appears as the original cradle of the ascetic ideal — the civilization whose Brahmanic and Buddhist traditions represent the purest, most ancient form of the will to nothingness that would later dominate Europe through Christianity.'

memes.label: Instinct
memes.description: "In Nietzsche's psychology, instinct is the pre-rational, life-affirming guiding force that morality and civilization work to suppress — the bodily wisdom that the ascetic ideal most fundamentally devalues."

memes.label: "Interpretation"
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s concept of interpretation as the fundamental activity of all life: every drive, instinct, and moral system is a form of interpretation and domination'

memes.label: Jesus of Nazareth
memes.description: 'Central figure of Christianity; Nietzsche treats Jesus as the symbolic origin point of the slave revolt in morality and the transvaluation of aristocratic values'

memes.label: Jews
memes.description: 'In Nietzsche''s Genealogy, the Jews are the "priestly nation" whose act of spiritual revenge — the revaluation of values — initiated the slave revolt in morals that Nietzsche traces across two millennia of Western history.'

memes.label: "Johann Joachim Winckelmann"
memes.description: 'German art historian and archaeologist whom Nietzsche groups with Goethe and Mozart as exemplars of the Hellenic awakening in the German spirit through contact with the South.'

memes.label: "Johann Sebastian Bach"
memes.description: 'German Baroque composer whose dedication of the High Mass Nietzsche cites as evidence of the artistʼs "slavish humility" before his public, illustrating the servile psychology inherent in the Christian artistic tradition.'

memes.label: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe
memes.description: 'German poet, playwright, and polymath (1749–1832) whom Nietzsche repeatedly invokes in the Genealogy as an exemplar of noble, life-affirming culture against the ascetic ideal.'

memes.label: "John Calvin"
memes.description: 'French theologian of the Reformation, invoked by Nietzsche alongside Luther as an exemplar of the conception of gods as "friends of spectacles of cruelty."'

memes.label: "Joseph Haydn"
memes.description: "Austrian composer (1732–1809) whose artistic disposition Nietzsche characterizes as that of a \"proud servant,\" contrasting it with Beethoven's \"proud peasant\" in the context of analyzing artistic servility"

memes.label: Justice
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s genealogical account of justice (Gerechtigkeit) as originating in the contractual creditor-debtor relationship, evolving from private vengeance into impersonal law through the increasing power of the community'

memes.label: Knowledge
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s critique of knowledge (Erkenntnis) as an expression of the ascetic ideal — the ''will to knowledge'' that, in its uncompromising devotion to truth at any cost, reveals itself as the latest and most refined form of the ascetic denial of life'

memes.label: "Kuno Fischer"
memes.description: 'German philosopher and historian of philosophy, cited by Nietzsche as a Spinoza commentator who misunderstood Spinoza on conscience'

memes.label: La Rochefoucauld
memes.description: 'French moralist and author of the Maxims (1613–1680) — Nietzsche places La Rochefoucauld in the select company of Plato, Spinoza, and Kant as one of four philosophers who, despite all their differences, were "united on one point: their contempt of pity."'

memes.label: Law
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s genealogical analysis of law (Recht) as a codification of the creditor-debtor relationship — not an expression of justice but a regularization of vengeance by the community, which grows more merciful as it grows more powerful'

memes.label: "Leonardo da Vinci"
memes.description: 'Italian Renaissance polymath whom Nietzsche singles out as the only artist with a "really super-Christian outlook," possessing an internal knowledge of the East and a silent, super-European character.'

memes.label: Leopold von Ranke
memes.description: 'German historian (1795–1886), founder of modern source-based historiography — Nietzsche satirizes Ranke as "that born classical advocate of every causa fortior, that cleverest of all the clever opportunists," the exemplar of a scholarly "objectivity" that is in fact prudent submission to power.'

memes.label: "Lord Byron"
memes.description: 'English Romantic poet whose burned autobiography Nietzsche cites as evidence of the incapacity of modern culture for truth about human beings.'

memes.label: "Ludwig Feuerbach"
memes.description: 'German philosopher of the Young Hegelian movement whose motto of "healthy sensuality" influenced the young Richard Wagner before his later turn to Christian themes.'

memes.label: Ludwig van Beethoven
memes.description: 'German composer (1770–1827) whom Nietzsche invokes in the Genealogy as a cultural exemplar — a figure of defiant, peasant-like pride whose music embodies the possibility of a "nobler culture" beyond the Christo-Raphaelite ascetic ideal.'

memes.label: Martin Luther
memes.description: 'German theologian and Reformation leader (1483–1546) whom Nietzsche treats in the Genealogy as a complex figure — both a liberator of the German spirit from Roman authority and an exemplar of the plebeian resentment that renewed Christianity rather than overcame it.'

memes.label: Master Morality
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s term for the original, spontaneous value system of the aristocratic caste — a morality of self-affirmation that calls its own qualities "good" and the qualities of the low "bad," prior to and independent of any reactive judgment.'

memes.label: "Memory"
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s concept of memory as a mnemotechnics of pain: the violent process by which the human animal was made calculable and capable of promising'

memes.label: Michael Angelo
memes.description: 'Italian Renaissance sculptor, painter, and architect (1475–1564) — Nietzsche rates him "higher than Raphael" for seeing "the ideal of a culture nobler than the Christo-Raphaelian," yet notes that he ultimately "broke down" before the "eternal feminine in Christianity."'

memes.label: Napoleon Bonaparte
memes.description: 'French military leader and emperor, described by Nietzsche as "the most unique and violent manifestation" of the will-to-power in modern history'

memes.label: Nazareth
memes.description: 'In Nietzsche''s Genealogy, Nazareth appears as the symbolic birthplace of the slave revolt in morals — the Galilean village whose "incarnate gospel of love" (Jesus) provided the figure around which the Jewish revaluation of values crystallized into Christianity.'

memes.label: "Nicolaus Copernicus"
memes.description: 'Polish astronomer whose heliocentric theory Nietzsche treats as the beginning of a "steep plane" of human self-belittling and existential diminishment.'

memes.label: "Noble"
memes.description: 'The noble mode of valuation in Nietzsche: the spontaneous self-affirmation of the ruling class that creates values through the pathos of distance'

memes.label: "Obligation"
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s analysis of obligation as rooted in the contractual debtor-creditor relationship: moral obligation is the internalized descendant of economic debt'

memes.label: Otto von Bismarck
memes.description: 'Prussian statesman and architect of German unification, cited by Nietzsche as an exemplar of aristocratic health and the will-to-power in modern political life'

memes.label: Pain
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s theory of pain as the most ancient mnemonic technique — the means by which memory is "burned in" to the human animal, and the foundation of the earliest contractual relationships, where the creditor''s right to inflict pain on the debtor constitutes the origin of punishment'

memes.label: Pathos of Distance
memes.description: 'The fundamental affective stance of the aristocratic soul: a felt sense of superiority, elevation, and separation from the lower orders, out of which genuine value-creation first becomes possible.'

memes.label: Paul Rée
memes.description: 'German philosopher (1849–1901), author of The Origin of the Moral Emotions (1877) — Nietzsche credits Rée as the initial stimulus for his own genealogical project and simultaneously positions him as the representative of "English" moral psychology whose every conclusion Nietzsche emphatically negates.'

memes.label: "Pericles"
memes.description: 'Athenian statesman whose funeral oration Nietzsche quotes in Essay 1 to illustrate the audacity and nonchalance of aristocratic races.'

memes.label: "Peter Paul Rubens"
memes.description: 'Flemish Baroque painter whom Nietzsche cites as an example of the artist who portrays his patrons according to their taste, not his own, illustrating the servility of most great artists.'

memes.label: "Philosopher"
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s analysis of the philosopher as a psychological type: the philosopher''s relationship to the ascetic ideal and the will to truth'

memes.label: Pity
memes.description: "Nietzsche's critique of pity as a covert form of power — the 'pity-morality' that appears selfless but actually extends the suffering it claims to relieve, functioning as the emotional core of the ascetic ideal's domination over life."

memes.label: Plato
memes.description: 'Ancient Greek philosopher (c. 428–348 BCE) whose metaphysical dualism and doctrine of the Forms represent, for Nietzsche in the Genealogy, the earliest philosophical form of the ascetic ideal — a life-denying will to truth that values the "true world" over the actual world.'

memes.label: "Priestly Caste"
memes.description: 'The priestly caste as a sociological type in the genealogy of morality: the origin of value-inversion and the slave revolt in morals'

memes.label: Priests
memes.description: 'In the Genealogy, the priestly caste is the agent of the slave revolt in morals — the weak who, through cunning and ressentiment, invert the aristocratic value system.'

memes.label: Promise
memes.description: "For Nietzsche, the capacity to make and keep promises is the defining achievement of civilization — the breeding of an animal that can answer for its own future, achieved through the mnemonotechnics of pain and punishment."

memes.label: Punishment
memes.description: 'In Nietzsche''s Genealogy, punishment is a complex historical phenomenon overlaid with multiple meanings: a creditor''s right to cruelty, a mnemonic technique for creating memory, and a site where the concept of guilt first crystallizes.'

memes.label: "Raphael"
memes.description: 'Italian Renaissance painter whom Nietzsche contrasts unfavorably with Michelangelo, describing Raphael as a modest glorifier of inherited values lacking the transgressive, value-creating instinct.'

memes.label: "Religion"
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s analysis of religion as a system of cruelty internalized: religions are at bottom systems of cruelties that give meaning to suffering'

memes.label: Renaissance
memes.description: 'In Nietzsche''s Genealogy, the Renaissance appears as the great counter-movement to Christianity — a "brilliantly sinister revival" of the classical aristocratic ideal that briefly threatened to overturn the slave revolt in morals before being defeated by the Reformation.'

memes.label: René Descartes
memes.description: 'French philosopher and mathematician (1596–1650) whose celibate life appears in the Genealogy as one entry in Nietzsche''s catalogue of the great unmarried philosophers — evidence that the ascetic ideal has governed the philosophical life from antiquity to modernity.'

memes.label: Responsibility
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s genealogical account of responsibility (Verantwortlichkeit) as the product of a long disciplinary process — the morality of custom — that bred an animal capable of making promises and answering for its own future'

memes.label: Ressentiment
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s signature concept: the creative revenge of the weak, who through imaginary vengeance invert the values of the strong, transforming powerlessness into moral superiority.'

memes.label: Revenge
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s analysis of revenge (Rache) as the driving force behind the slave revolt in morality — an "imaginary revenge" that the powerless take through the revaluation of values, transforming their weakness into virtue and the strength of their oppressors into evil'

memes.label: Richard Wagner
memes.description: 'German composer (1813–1883) who appears in the Genealogy as a case study of the artist''s submission to the ascetic ideal — once Nietzsche''s mentor, later his prime example of cultural decadence.'

memes.label: Rome
memes.description: 'In Nietzsche''s Genealogy, Rome symbolizes the classical aristocratic ideal in its purest form — the embodiment of noble self-affirmation that stands in mortal opposition to Judaea''s slave revolt in morals, and whose defeat marks the decisive turning point in Western moral history.'

memes.label: Rudolf Virchow
memes.description: 'German physician, pathologist, and anthropologist (1821–1902) — Nietzsche contests the ethnographical claim of Virchow that dark-haired populations in Germany derive from Celtic ancestry, arguing instead for a pre-Aryan origin, in a passage illustrating the racial subtext of aristocratic "good" and "bad" in Essay 1.'

memes.label: "Sacrifice"
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s analysis of sacrifice as the foundation of religion and morality: the human capacity for self-cruelty transformed into sacred practice'

memes.label: "Saint Paul"
memes.description: 'Apostle to the Gentiles, cited by Nietzsche as one of "three Jews and one Jewess" representing the triumph of Judaeo-Christian values over classical Rome'

memes.label: Science
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s analysis of modern science (Wissenschaft) as the latest and most refined form of the ascetic ideal — not its opponent but its heir, sharing the same unconditional faith in truth and the same life-denying impulse disguised as objectivity'

memes.label: William Shakespeare
memes.description: 'English playwright and poet (1564–1616) whose works Nietzsche references in his critique of Christian asceticism and sacerdotal corruption of taste.'

memes.label: "Shame"
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s analysis of shame as a social-psychological mechanism: shame is the internalized gaze of the community, a product of the morality of custom rather than an innate moral feeling'

memes.label: Slave Morality
memes.description: 'In the Genealogy, slave morality is the reactive value system created through ressentiment — defining "good" as the opposite of the powerful, and "evil" as the powerful themselves — which Nietzsche traces from its Jewish origins through Christianity to modern democratic values.'

memes.label: Socrates
memes.description: 'Athenian philosopher (c. 470–399 BCE) whose marriage — undertaken, Nietzsche claims, ironice — serves in the Genealogy as the comic exception that proves the ascetic rule: no great philosopher has ever been a husband in earnest.'

memes.label: Sovereign Individual
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s concept of the "sovereign individual" — the ripest fruit of the long moral training of humankind, who possesses the right to make promises and whose conscience is a source of pride rather than torment'

memes.label: State
memes.description: 'In Nietzsche''s Genealogy, the State is theorized as originating in an act of primordial violence — a "terrible tyranny" that crushes pre-state communities and creates the conditions for the internalization of human instincts and the birth of bad conscience.'

memes.label: Stendhal
memes.description: 'French novelist (1783–1842) whom Nietzsche admired as a psychological realist and cited for his aesthetic theory that beauty is "a promise of happiness."'

memes.label: Suffering
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s analysis of suffering (Leiden) as the fundamental human problem — not suffering itself, which is inevitable, but the meaninglessness of suffering, which the ascetic ideal exploits by providing an interpretation that makes suffering into guilt and thereby bearable'

memes.label: "Tertullian"
memes.description: 'Early Christian apologist whose vision of the damned suffering in hell is quoted by Nietzsche as evidence of Christian cruelty and ressentiment'

memes.label: The Reformation
memes.description: 'In the Genealogy, the Reformation appears as a decisive event in European moral history — a resurgence of the slave revolt in morals that, under Luther, revived the ascetic ideal at the very moment the Renaissance seemed poised to overcome it.'

memes.label: Theognis
memes.description: 'Megarian poet cited by Nietzsche in "On the Genealogy of Morality" as the mouthpiece of aristocratic value judgments, exemplifying the master morality distinction between "good" and "bad."'

memes.label: "Thomas Aquinas"
memes.description: 'Medieval scholastic theologian (1225–1274) whom Nietzsche quotes in the First Essay as an authority on the cruelty of the Christian vision of Paradise — where the blessed watch the damned suffer to increase their own bliss'

memes.label: "Thomas Henry Huxley"
memes.description: 'British biologist and defender of Darwin whose reproach to Herbert Spencer of "administrative Nihilism" Nietzsche cites in Essay 2, before arguing that the problem of modern science runs far deeper than mere administration.'

memes.label: "Thomas Moore"
memes.description: 'Irish poet and songwriter (1779–1852) whom Nietzsche references in the Third Essay as an example of an author "too good" to write an honest autobiography'

memes.label: "Thucydides"
memes.description: 'Ancient Greek historian whose moral outlook Nietzsche invokes in Peoples and Countries as exemplifying the aristocratic ethos of the governing castes, contrasted with modern Christian morality.'

memes.label: "Transvaluation"
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s concept of transvaluation (Umwertung aller Werte): the radical inversion of values accomplished by slave morality and the counter-inversion Nietzsche seeks to enact'

memes.label: Van Dyck
memes.description: 'Flemish Baroque painter (1599–1641), court painter to Charles I — Nietzsche presents Van Dyck as the exemplary "nobler" artist who "did not descend from himself, but rather lifted up others to himself" in his portraiture, a model of aristocratic artistic value-creation.'

memes.label: "Virtue"
memes.description: 'Nietzsche''s critique of virtue: what is called virtue is often sublimated cruelty, the self-deception of the weak, or the internalized violence of the morality of custom'

memes.label: Will to Power
memes.description: 'In the Genealogy, the will to power is the fundamental driving force of all life — not merely a desire to dominate but the active, shaping, interpreting force through which all phenomena acquire meaning, form, and function.'

memes.label: Will to Truth
memes.description: "Nietzsche's critical concept: the unconditional drive to pursue truth at all costs, which he diagnoses as a disguised expression of the ascetic ideal."

memes.label: "Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart"
memes.description: 'Austrian composer whom Nietzsche groups with Winckelmann and Goethe as exemplars of the Hellenic potential in the German spirit, awakened by contact with the South.'

memes.label: "Xaver Doudan"
memes.description: 'French writer and critic (1800–1872) whose notion of the "ravages" of existence is cited by Nietzsche in the Third Essay in his analysis of the ascetic ideal'

memes.label: Zarathustra
memes.description: "Ancient Persian prophet and the namesake of Nietzsche's philosophical protagonist in *Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, invoked at the close of the Genealogy as 'Zarathustra the godless.'"
